_description: Site-Specific Geology retroactive enrichment - adds geological hazards and site characterization to existing frames

_physics_added.regolith_bearing_failure: Foundation failure risk from insufficient bearing capacity (300-400 kPa Mars baseline from Phoenix)
_physics_added.subsurface_ice_instability: Ground ice thermal cycles create foundation and extraction challenges (<2m depth critical)
_physics_added.enhanced_perchlorate_corrosion: Site-specific perchlorate concentrations (0.4-0.6 wt% baseline) cause enhanced equipment corrosion
_physics_added.slope_instability_hazard: Terrain slopes >7° create construction and operational safety challenges
_physics_added.geological_isru_inefficiency: Site geology reduces ISRU efficiency below laboratory conditions
_physics_added.cumulative_geological_stress: Long-term mission geological stress accumulates over multiple sols

hazards.description: Early mission geological survey reveals suboptimal regolith for water extraction
Higher than expected perchlorate concentrations accelerate equipment corrosion
Shallow ice creates foundation instability during thermal cycling
Infrastructure load approaches bearing capacity limits of local regolith
Equipment placement on marginal slopes creates operational challenges
Geological heterogeneity affects multiple ISRU systems
Cumulative perchlorate exposure creates system degradation
Very shallow ice creates significant foundation challenges
Expanding infrastructure exceeds local geological bearing capacity
Construction on steeper terrain requires enhanced slope stabilization
Complex geology affects all ISRU operations
High perchlorate zone causes severe corrosion damage
Extreme thermal cycles affect ice-rich regolith stability
Long-term loading causes regolith consolidation and bearing failure risk
Advanced construction on unstable terrain creates significant hazards
Mature mission encounters challenging geological variations
Long-term perchlorate exposure causes significant system degradation
Maximum infrastructure load approaches critical geological failure threshold
Extremely shallow ice creates major foundation instability
